question: What are two advantages of using an Agile project management tool? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Consolidate project artifacts to a common repository.
optionB: Better relationships with business stakeholders.
optionC: Improve governance with gate steps in development.
optionD: Increased visibility into sprint and project status.
explanation: Agile project management tools (Jira, Azure DevOps, etc.) deliver two core advantages: (A) they centralize artifacts — user stories, acceptance criteria, attachments, links, decisions — in one repository accessible to the whole team; (D) they provide live visibility into sprint progress through burndown charts, velocity, and Kanban boards so the team and stakeholders always know status. Better stakeholder relationships (B) and gate-based governance (C) are not what these tools primarily deliver.

question: What are two roles a project Steering Committee plays in determining what methodologies are used? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Designing a methodology that will meet a particular project's requirements.
optionB: Approving deviations from the chosen methodology, when required to address project issues.
optionC: Enforcing that corporate project stage gates are part of the chosen methodology.
optionD: Setting the criteria for selecting Agile or Waterfall methodology to be used on internal projects.
explanation: A steering committee operates at a governance level. It (D) sets the policy/criteria the organization uses to choose between methodologies (Agile vs. Waterfall) for any given project, and (B) approves exceptions/deviations from that policy when a specific project needs to depart from the standard. The committee does not design methodologies (A) or enforce specific gate steps (C) at the implementation level — those are project-management or PMO activities.

question: At Universal Containers, Salesforce administrators are making changes to permission sets under instruction from the business. Randomly, various SOQL statements are failing. What strategy could be advised to bring this issue to the developer's attention earlier?
optionA: Ask administrators to only make changes to profiles instead.
optionB: Create a sandbox refresh strategy to ensure each sandbox is refreshed every day.
optionC: Extract each permission set, commit and merge to source control, and run through CI checks.
optionD: Advise developers to switch to SOSL queries that are more robust instead.
explanation: Treating permission sets as source-controlled metadata catches the problem at the right time: when the change is committed, the CI pipeline runs unit tests, finds the SOQL failures (because the test runs as a user with the new perms), and surfaces them to the developer before they reach production. Profiles vs. permission sets (A) and SOSL (D) don't address the visibility gap; daily refreshes (B) destroy in-progress dev work.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has used Salesforce for the last 6 years with 50% custom code. UC has recently implemented continuous integration. UC wants to improve old test classes whenever new functionality invalidates tests. UC also wants to reduce the deployment time required. What should the architect recommend?
optionA: Do not execute any test classes in sandboxes and Production.
optionB: Execute all test classes in sandboxes and selected test classes in Production.
optionC: Do not execute test classes in sandboxes and all test classes in Production.
optionD: Test classes cannot be executed in sandboxes.
explanation: In CI sandboxes, run the full test suite to catch regressions and continuously surface tests that need updating. For production deployments, use 'Run Specified Tests' (only the tests covering the components being deployed) — a much smaller set than 'Run Local Tests' or 'Run All Tests' — which dramatically reduces deployment time while still meeting the 75% coverage requirement.

question: Universal Containers has five development teams. The performance of the teams has been good, but the number of bugs has been increasing. After each sprint, they need more time to understand the code and make changes. What are two ways to improve the performance? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Sprint review process.
optionB: Define a team that will analyze/approve all changes.
optionC: Define and follow code standards.
optionD: Version control system to identify who is generating the bugs.
explanation: (C) Code standards (naming, structure, comments, patterns) make code predictable and faster to read across teams — directly addressing the 'need more time to understand the code' symptom. (A) Sprint reviews (and retrospectives) surface defect patterns and process issues so teams can adjust. A central approval team (B) creates a bottleneck; using version control to assign blame (D) is a culture anti-pattern.

question: Universal Containers is a global organization that maintains regional production instances of Salesforce. One region has created a new application to track shipping containers. The CIO has requested that this new application be used globally by all the Salesforce instances and further maintained and modified regionally by local administrators. Which two deployment tools will support the request? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Ant Migration Tool
optionB: Developer Console
optionC: VS Code with Salesforce Extension
optionD: Change Sets
explanation: Change sets (D) only work between sandboxes and a production org with a deployment connection — they cannot ship metadata across unrelated production orgs in different regions. Metadata API tooling — the Ant Migration Tool (A) and the Salesforce Extensions for VS Code / SFDX (C) — work against any org via API, so they can deploy the same package to every regional production org.

question: Universal Containers has just completed several projects, including new custom objects and custom fields. Administrators are having difficulty maintaining the application due to not knowing how objects and fields are being used. Which two options should an architect recommend? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Create design standards to consistently use the description field on custom objects.
optionB: Create design standards with a document to store all custom objects and custom fields.
optionC: Create design standards to consistently use the description field on custom fields.
optionD: Create design standards to require help text on all custom fields and custom objects.
optionE: Create design standards to require all custom fields on all custom object page layouts.
explanation: Description fields on objects (A) and fields (C) are stored in metadata, travel with deployments, and are the canonical place for admins/devs to capture intent and usage — they appear in Setup, in tooling, and in package.xml. Help text (D) is user-facing and not the right channel for maintenance docs. External documents (B) drift out of sync. Layout-required fields (E) is unrelated.

question: Universal Containers is adopting Scrum as an Agile methodology and wants to choose a software tool to support the adoption. What three key features of an Agile development support tool should an architect look for? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Work (for example, user stories or tasks) assignment.
optionB: Email notifications when work is created or changed.
optionC: Sprint backlog management.
optionD: Product backlog prioritization.
explanation: Scrum-specific essentials are (D) a prioritized product backlog, (C) a sprint backlog populated from it, and (A) the ability to assign work items to team members. Kanban boards (E) belong to a different framework, and email notifications (B) are a generic feature of almost any system, not a Scrum-specific need.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is considering implementing a minor change policy for a series of low-risk user stories that are commonly received by the UC admins. The policy would allow admins to make these changes directly in production. UC does not have continuous integration/continuous delivery (CI/CD) in place. Which three best practices should the architect suggest UC follow for their new change policy? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Downstream environments will not be automatically updated when production changes.
optionB: Minor changes do not need to be documented and can be made at any time.
optionC: Minor changes should be thoroughly documented and follow some type of standard cadence.
optionD: All changes should still be tested.
optionE: CI/CD is required to successfully manage minor changes.
explanation: Even for low-risk changes the team should: (D) test before promoting; (C) document them and follow a standard cadence to keep change visible and predictable; and (A) acknowledge that downstream sandboxes will drift from production unless explicitly refreshed/back-filled. (B) is the opposite of best practice; (E) is false — small change policies can run without full CI/CD.

question: Universal Containers requires that all sandboxes that have not been recently refreshed must also receive the newest changes to production. This must be done before any functionality from that environment can be moved to production. Which deployment tool would allow this deployment process to be managed in an automated fashion?
optionA: Change Sets
optionB: Workbench
optionC: Force.com Migration Tool (Ant)
optionD: Force.com IDE
explanation: The Ant-based Force.com Migration Tool (and modern SFDX equivalents) is scriptable and can be invoked from CI pipelines, making it the only listed tool that supports an automated, repeatable 'sync this sandbox with production before deploying outbound' workflow. Change sets are manual, point-and-click; Workbench and Force.com IDE aren't designed for unattended automation.

question: Universal Containers has seven orgs in different regions. Its processes are global and standardized but each region needs the flexibility to be able to understand the global code and customize some aspects for its regions. Which development model is optimized for this need?
optionA: Create a centralized Git with all the code where the global team approves the changes made by the local teams.
optionB: Use unlocked packages to deploy the global code and allow each country to create its own customized unlocked package extensions.
optionC: Use a managed package for global code and another managed package for all regions' code.
optionD: Use a managed package to deploy the global code and allow local teams to request the addition of code within that package.
explanation: Unlocked packages with extension packages are the package-development pattern Salesforce designed for exactly this scenario: a base unlocked package carrying the global, standardized code/config, plus per-region 'extension' unlocked packages that depend on the base and add region-specific customization. Each region deploys base + its own extension, can read/extend the base, and is in control of its extension's lifecycle.

question: Universal Containers is concerned that after each release, reports and dashboards seem to roll back to previous versions. Executives spend many hours crafting these dashboards to perfectly meet their needs, and are now questioning the Salesforce platform's ability to save things, even data records. What can the Salesforce architect advise to stop the rollbacks from happening?
optionA: Ensure report metadata is exported daily and that it is merged into the developer branches before the next release.
optionB: Use a third-party data warehouse.
optionC: Remove the executives' ability to change reports, and only allow developers to do that.
optionD: Back up all the reports just before the release, then reimport them after the release.
explanation: Reports and dashboards are metadata. If executives edit them in production but the development pipeline still carries the older versions in source, every deployment overwrites their changes. Pulling the latest report metadata from production into the source branches regularly (and merging) keeps the source of truth aligned with what executives have built, eliminating the rollback.

question: Universal Containers has recently acquired another business that uses Salesforce extensively. UC wants to merge their Salesforce orgs to effectively sell and service customers under one business. Traditionally, UC has followed an Agile development methodology to deliver Salesforce functionality. With the merging businesses, UC is convinced that adopting a Waterfall development methodology is the best approach. Which are two positive aspects of using a Waterfall development methodology? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Changes late in the process are expected and can be handled by integrating them into the requirements specs.
optionB: Milestones, timelines, and estimates tend to be more accurate and predictable due to the upfront due diligence.
optionC: The costs of starting the project are low since much of the design work is pushed to later stages of the process.
optionD: Complex processes that will need to be built are thoroughly understood and documented before coding begins.
explanation: Waterfall's strengths come from heavy upfront analysis: (D) complex processes are fully analyzed and documented before any code is written, and (B) those upfront artifacts produce more reliable timelines, milestones, and cost estimates. (A) is the Agile virtue, not Waterfall; (C) reverses Waterfall — design costs are front-loaded.

question: When replacing an old legacy system with Salesforce, which two strategies should the plan consider to mitigate the risks associated with migrating data from the legacy system to Salesforce? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Migrate users in phases based on their functions, requiring parallel use of legacy system and Salesforce for a certain period of time.
optionB: Identify the data relevant to the new system, including dependencies, and develop a plan/scripts for verification of data integrity.
optionC: Use a full sandbox environment for all the systems involved, a full deployment plan with test data generation scripts, and full testing including integrations.
optionD: Use a full sandbox environment and perform test runs of data migration scripts/processes with real data from the legacy system.
explanation: Two concrete risk-reducers: (B) scope the data, model the dependencies, and write verification scripts to prove integrity post-load; (D) rehearse the migration end-to-end in a Full sandbox using actual legacy data so that timing, transformations, and edge cases are flushed out before go-live. Phased user migration (A) is a cutover strategy, not a data-migration risk control; using test data (C) hides real-data issues.

question: Universal Containers is delivering many changes to its Salesforce system. Adoption reports are discovering that many features are unused. The steering committee wants this to change and is looking to the architect for advice. What should an architect recommend to overcome this?
optionA: Using Lightning Web Components for every user interface.
optionB: Sending weekly communication emails reporting on least engaged users.
optionC: Adopting user-centered design to understand user needs before building the solution.
optionD: Stop development until current features start being used.
explanation: Low adoption usually means features are being built that don't match what users actually need or how they work. User-centered design (interviews, journey mapping, prototyping with real users, usability testing) shifts the discovery process upstream so what gets shipped is what users will actually use.

question: Universal Containers is in the final stages of building a new application to track custom containers. During a review of the application, a business subject matter expert mentioned that it would be nice to be able to track additional container types beyond what was originally scoped during the plan and design phase. Which two actions should be performed to mitigate the risk? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Have a discussion with the business SME and communicate that a new developer environment will be needed to mitigate the risk.
optionB: Have a discussion with the business SME and communicate that Salesforce has limitations in supporting such a feature to mitigate the risk.
optionC: Escalate and communicate to stakeholders the risk and mitigate it by allocating additional resources to support the new requirement based on stakeholder input.
optionD: Escalate and communicate to stakeholders the risk and mitigate it by extending the timeline of the project to support the new requirement based on stakeholder input.
explanation: Late-arriving scope is a classic project risk. The right response is to escalate it to stakeholders with an impact assessment and offer them the two standard scope-management levers: (C) add resources or (D) extend the timeline (or descope something else). Either choice belongs to the stakeholders, not the project team, which is why escalation is the central action.

question: Universal Containers is planning to release simple configuration changes and enhancements to their Sales Cloud. A technical architect recommends using change sets. Which two advantages would change sets provide in this scenario? Choose 2 answers
optionA: The ability to deploy a very large number of components easily.
optionB: A simple and declarative method for deployment.
optionC: An easy way to deploy related components.
optionD: The ability to track changes to components.
explanation: For small declarative releases, change sets shine: (B) they are point-and-click — no scripting, no command line — and (C) the 'View/Add Dependencies' button helps roll up related components into one set. They are NOT good at large component counts (A) or change tracking/history (D) — those require source-driven tooling.

question: Universal Containers has development teams working on multiple projects. They are exploring a source control tool to track and manage their code/config. What two benefits does a source control tool provide? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Provides the ability for distributed teams to work in isolation.
optionB: Provides automated code/configuration deployments.
optionC: Provides the ability to automatically identify issues in code/configuration.
optionD: Provides the ability to back up code/configuration changes.
explanation: Source control's two core benefits: (A) feature branches let distributed teams work independently and merge their changes back together; (D) every commit is a recoverable snapshot, so all code/config history is backed up and reversible. Automated deployment (B) and static analysis (C) are CI/CD and code-quality tools layered on top of source control, not source control itself.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has two major releases every year and the team always runs into longer deployment times. In which two ways can UC reduce deployment time? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Specify the tests to run by using the RunSpecifiedTests test level.
optionB: Use recent deployment validations and the Quick Deploy feature.
optionC: Validate the deployment before migrating components to production.
optionD: Deploy components in groups to reduce deployment time.
explanation: Two big levers: (A) RunSpecifiedTests runs only the tests that cover the deployed components (instead of all org tests), drastically cutting test execution time; (B) a recent (within 4 days) successful Validate-Only deployment qualifies for Quick Deploy, which skips re-running tests and re-validation entirely on the actual deploy. Plain validation (C) doesn't shorten the eventual deploy unless paired with Quick Deploy. Splitting into groups (D) generally increases total elapsed time.

question: Which two statements are accurate about why Mock objects are needed when writing test classes? Choose 2 answers
optionA: A Mock is needed whenever the code makes an HTTP callout.
optionB: Mock can also be used on the classes that extend the Batchable interface to bypass the batch jobs.
optionC: Some methods invoke long-running processes; using Mock is a shortcut for bypassing the long executions.
optionD: Using a Mock allows the test class to bypass the dependencies of other objects, methods, state, or behaviors. Therefore, the developer has total control over their own code.
explanation: (A) Apex tests can't make real HTTP callouts, so HttpCalloutMock (or Test.setMock) is required for any code path that makes a callout. (D) Mocking lets the developer isolate the unit under test from external dependencies, deterministically setting up inputs/outputs and asserting against them — the foundation of true unit testing.

question: Universal Containers is looking to construct a continuous integration process to help manage code quality. Which three tools should be used to enable this? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Force.com Migration Tool (or SFDX CLI)
optionB: Continuous Integration build tool
optionC: Project management tool
optionD: Full sandbox environment
explanation: CI requires three building blocks: (E) source control to capture every change, (B) a CI build tool (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps Pipelines, etc.) to run the pipeline, and (A) a deployment tool (Force.com Migration Tool / SFDX CLI) to push metadata into target orgs and run tests. A project management tool (C) supports planning but is not part of CI; full sandboxes (D) are useful but not required to run CI.

question: Which are two recommended methods of creating test data in Salesforce? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Utilize Heroku Connect to provide test class data.
optionB: Reference data from middleware directly within your test class.
optionC: Host a mock endpoint to produce sample information from an endpoint.
optionD: Load a CSV as a static resource and reference it in a test class.
explanation: Apex tests are isolated by default and shouldn't depend on real org data. (D) A static-resource CSV plus Test.loadData() is the documented pattern for seeding bulk test records. (C) For test classes that exercise integration code, a mock HTTP endpoint (HttpCalloutMock) provides the response data deterministically. Heroku Connect (A) and live middleware references (B) make tests slow and non-deterministic.

question: Universal Containers' (UC) Customer Community is scheduled to go live in the EMEA region in 3 months. UC follows a typical centralized governance model. Two weeks ago, the project stakeholders informed the project team about recent changes in mandatory compliance requirements needed to go live. The project team analyzed the requirements and have estimated additional budget needs of 30% of the project cost for incorporating the compliance requirements. Which management team is empowered to approve this additional budget?
optionA: Change Control Board
optionB: Executive Steering Committee
optionC: Project Management Committee
optionD: Security Review Committee
explanation: A 30% budget increase is a strategic, financial decision well above project-level authority. The Executive Steering Committee — with the senior sponsors and budget owners — is the body empowered to approve additional funding. CCBs handle scope/change control inside an approved budget; PMOs run delivery; security reviews approve security posture, not money.

question: Universal Containers (UC) just started configuration and customization of its Salesforce organization. The architect suggested the definition of an Application Lifecycle Management (ALM) process. What are three benefits of following an ALM process? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Training new users after each minor and major release.
optionB: Releasing new features on a consistent schedule.
optionC: Preventing existing working functionality from stopping.
optionD: Avoiding defects from being deployed to production.
optionE: Defining metrics for application development project success.
explanation: ALM brings predictability and quality: (B) consistent release cadence, (C) regression protection so existing functionality keeps working, and (D) defect prevention through staged testing and gated promotion. End-user training (A) is a change-management/adoption activity that ALM enables but is not itself a benefit of ALM. Defining success metrics (E) is governance, not ALM.

question: Universal Containers has recently replaced a custom service center application with Salesforce Service Cloud. Administrators are now confused about which fields and classes are actively being utilized and how future implementations can be maintained. Choose 2 answers
optionA: Create an adoption plan for end users using Salesforce dashboards and reports.
optionB: Create a governance framework focused on high-level business strategy and goals.
optionC: Create a design standard requiring integration to use declarative configuration patterns.
optionD: Create a governance process requiring all projects to create project deliverable documentation.
optionE: Create a standard method for deprecating classes and fields using design standards.
explanation: (D) Required project documentation gives admins a written record of what each component is for and how it's used. (E) A formal deprecation standard — naming, marking, communicating, eventually removing unused metadata — keeps the org from accumulating stale fields and classes that nobody dares touch. Together they directly address the 'what's actually being used?' confusion.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has two subsidiaries which operate independently. UC has made the decision to operate two separate Salesforce orgs, one for each subsidiary. However, certain functions and processes between the two orgs must be standardized. Which approach should UC take to develop customizations once and make them available in both orgs?
optionA: Develop the functionality in a sandbox and deploy it to both production orgs.
optionB: Create a managed (or unlocked) package in a Developer Edition org and deploy it to both production orgs.
optionC: Create change sets in a sandbox and deploy them to both production orgs.
optionD: Set up Salesforce-to-Salesforce to deploy the functionality from one org to the other.
explanation: When the same package of functionality must live in two unrelated production orgs and stay synchronized over time, packages (managed or unlocked) built in a Developer Edition / Dev Hub org are the right tool. They let you version, ship, and upgrade the same component set into multiple orgs. Sandboxes (A) and change sets (C) only deploy to their parent production org. Salesforce-to-Salesforce (D) shares records, not metadata.

question: Universal Containers has many development teams deploying into a single org. The business is very seasonal and approaching its busiest season. The business owner asks the architect for advice about its next major production release. What best practice should an architect recommend?
optionA: Make declarative changes in production only.
optionB: Bypass regression testing for minor changes.
optionC: Developers should conduct user acceptance testing.
optionD: Avoid releasing near peak business periods.
explanation: Approaching peak season, the risk of an issue is amplified — a defect that would normally be a nuisance becomes a revenue/SLA event. Best practice is to freeze major changes and avoid releases close to peak business windows. (A) and (B) are anti-patterns; (C) violates the principle that UAT must be done by business users, not developers.

question: What sandbox type would be appropriate for diagnosing reports of poor performance when accessing certain Visualforce pages?
optionA: Developer Sandbox
optionB: Developer Pro Sandbox
optionC: Full Sandbox
optionD: Partial Copy Sandbox
explanation: Performance issues are usually data-volume- and integration-driven. Only a Full Sandbox includes a full copy of production data and is sized like production, allowing realistic reproduction and profiling of slow Visualforce pages. Smaller sandbox types don't carry enough data to surface the same query plans, view-state sizes, or render times.

question: Universal Containers has a complex deployment coming up. The deployment will include several Apex classes which depend on custom settings that hold important configuration. How should an architect manage this deployment?
optionA: Manually deploy and populate the custom settings in production prior to the Apex class deployment.
optionB: Migrate the configuration to a Custom Metadata Type and include it in the deployment to production.
optionC: Manually deploy and populate custom settings in production using a change set.
optionD: Script the deployment of all functionality via the Force.com Migration Tool.
explanation: Custom Metadata Types are deployable as metadata — both definitions AND records ship in change sets, packages, and Metadata API deployments. Custom Setting records, by contrast, are data and must be populated separately (often manually). Migrating the configuration to Custom Metadata Types makes the entire deployment atomic and repeatable.

question: A Salesforce administrator has initiated a deployment using a change set. The deployment has taken more time than usual. What is the potential reason for this?
optionA: The change set performance is independent of included components.
optionB: The change set includes field type changes for some objects.
optionC: The change set includes new custom objects and custom fields.
optionD: The change set includes changes to permission sets and profiles.
explanation: Field data type changes (e.g., Number → Text) force Salesforce to scan and convert every existing record's value plus revalidate every dependent formula, validation rule, and Apex class — by far the most common cause of unexpectedly long change-set deployments. New objects/fields (C) and permission set changes (D) do not require row-by-row data conversion.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has multiple teams working on different projects. Multiple projects will be deployed to many production orgs. During code reviews, the architect finds inconsistently named variables and lack of best practices. What should an architect recommend to improve consistency?
optionA: Create a Center of Excellence for release management.
optionB: Use static code analysis to enforce coding standards.
optionC: Require pull requests to be reviewed by two developers before merging.
optionD: Execute regression testing before code can be committed.
explanation: Static code analysis tools (PMD, Apex PMD, CodeScan) automatically and consistently enforce naming conventions, complexity limits, and best-practice patterns on every commit, scaling across many teams without depending on individual reviewer diligence. Two-reviewer PR rules (C) help but are inconsistent across reviewers; regression tests (D) catch behavior bugs, not style/standards.

question: Universal Containers is building a custom application on the Force.com platform. There is a budget and release date that has been set by the board of directors, but the application must meet the requirements that will be submitted and voted on by a public user community. What is the risk associated with the scenario?
optionA: The project is not using the Waterfall methodology.
optionB: The requirements should not be solicited by an external community.
optionC: The requirements are unknown and the release date has been set.
optionD: The project is not using an Agile methodology.
explanation: The classic project-risk triangle: scope, time, cost. Time and cost are fixed (board-set), but scope is unknown and won't be defined until a community votes. Without flex on at least one corner, delivery risk is severe. The chosen methodology (A/D) is a downstream decision; soliciting requirements externally (B) is fine in itself.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a customized repository that represents lots of different apps or projects. UC currently is trying to shift from the org development model to the package development model to manage changes. In the org development model, each developer starts their work within their own personal sandbox. When it comes to choosing development environments, what should a Salesforce architect recommend?
optionA: Start using scratch orgs that track all changes automatically and proceed with a staggered approach since scratch orgs can coexist with other models.
optionB: Start using scratch orgs because a developer can spin up a scratch org to start a new project, start a new feature branch, or start automated testing.
optionC: Keep developing in dev sandboxes, so that the developers feel no impact at all as they are used to sandbox development.
optionD: Keep developing in dev sandboxes because scratch orgs are not within the code deployment path.
explanation: The package development model is built around scratch orgs: source-driven, disposable, defined by config files, and ideal for spinning up a fresh org per feature branch or CI build. They give every developer a clean, reproducible environment that exactly matches the package's source — the central enabler of the package development model.

question: Universal Containers has written several validation rules and workflow rules for the Lead object. Which two test types should an architect suggest to ensure that a large inbound call center does not experience platform slowdowns under high call volume for the Lead object? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Unit Test
optionB: Performance Test
optionC: Load Test
optionD: Stress Test
explanation: (C) Load testing simulates the expected high call volume and measures whether throughput and response times remain acceptable. (B) Performance testing complements it by measuring per-transaction speed under that load. Stress testing pushes beyond expected limits to find break points and is generally not allowed without coordinating with Salesforce. Unit tests measure correctness, not performance.

question: Universal Containers is starting a Center of Excellence (CoE). Which two user groups should an architect recommend to join the CoE? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Inside Sales Users
optionB: Call Center Agents
optionC: Executive Sponsors
optionD: Program Team
explanation: A CoE needs governance authority and execution capability. (C) Executive Sponsors provide strategic direction, funding, and unblock cross-org issues. (D) The Program Team brings architects, leads, and project managers who run the day-to-day governance. Frontline users (A, B) are stakeholders to be served, not CoE members.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has recently acquired other companies that have their own Salesforce orgs. These companies have been merged as new UC business units. The CEO has requested an architect review the org strategy taking into consideration two main factors: (1) The CEO wants business process standardization among all business units. (2) Business process integration is not required as the different business units have different customers and expertise. Which org strategy should the architect recommend in this scenario, and why?
optionA: Single-org strategy, as costs increase as the number of orgs go up.
optionB: Multi-org strategy, as it is uncommon for diversified business units to get used to working in the same space as other business units.
optionC: Single-org strategy, as the high level of business process standardization will be easier to implement in a single org.
optionD: Multi-org strategy, as they could deploy a common managed (or unlocked) package into the orgs of the different business units.
explanation: The two stated facts point opposite ways: standardization argues for consolidation, but BUs share no customers/processes (no integration need), which removes the main benefit of single-org. A multi-org strategy with a shared package gives each BU autonomy on its own data while a common package enforces standardization of the processes — best of both.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is embarked on a large Salesforce transformation journey. UC's DevOps team raised a question about tracking Salesforce metadata throughout the development lifecycle across sandboxes all the way to production. As the deployment architect of the project, what should be the recommendation to track which version of each feature is in different environments?
optionA: Use an AppExchange or third-party tool that is specialized in Salesforce deployment.
optionB: Use change sets to track deployed customizations.
optionC: Use an Excel sheet to track deployment steps and document the SFDX commands.
optionD: Use Salesforce SFDX commands to deploy to different sandboxes.
explanation: Visibility into 'which version of which feature is in which environment' is exactly what specialized DevOps tools (Copado, Gearset, Flosum, AutoRABIT) are built for: pipeline dashboards, environment maps, version diffs, deployment history, and rollback. Change sets (B) and raw SFDX (D) are deployment mechanisms; spreadsheets (C) drift instantly out of sync.

question: Universal Containers' (UC) development team is using an Agile tool to track the status of build items, but only in terms of stages. UC is not able to track any effort estimates, log any hours worked, or keep track of remaining effort. For what reasons should UC consider using the Agile tool for effort tracking?
optionA: Allows the management team to manage the performance of bad developers who are slacking off.
optionB: Allows the developer to compare their effort estimates and actuals to better adjust their future estimates.
optionC: Allows the organization to track the developer's work hours for salary compensation purposes.
optionD: Allows the management team to make critical timeline commitments based solely on developer estimates.
explanation: Effort tracking in Agile exists to improve the team's estimation accuracy over time — comparing estimates to actuals lets the team calibrate, develop a stable velocity, and forecast better. (A) and (C) misuse estimation as performance/HR control, an Agile anti-pattern; (D) makes timeline commitments without slack — a planning anti-pattern.

question: The CTO at Universal Containers is complaining to the software development managers that he has no visibility of their teams' work status. What two software development methodologies should an architect suggest to solve this issue, and why? Choose 2 answers
optionA: DevOps, because monitoring and logging practices help you stay informed of performance in real time.
optionB: Kanban, because one of its basic elements is to make everything visible, creating consistent transparency of work items.
optionC: Scrum, because openness is one of the five core Scrum values.
optionD: Waterfall, because it defines a fixed schedule and duration for each activity.
explanation: Both Kanban (B) and Scrum (C) are explicitly built around visibility: Kanban's board makes every work item and its column instantly visible; Scrum lists openness as a core value and uses sprint reviews, daily standups, and product/sprint backlogs to surface status. Waterfall (D) defines schedules but offers little ongoing visibility into progress; DevOps (A) is about deployment performance, not work visibility.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is planning for a huge data migration as part of their Service Cloud implementation. UC has approximately 15 million customers, 30 million contacts, and 30 million active cases. Which two key areas of UC's data migration test plan should be included? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Target Salesforce server and source system IP addresses.
optionB: Manual and automated data validation approaches.
optionC: Success criteria for data migration.
optionD: APIs to be used for data migration.
explanation: A test plan needs (C) explicit success criteria — what 'good' looks like (record counts match, key fields populated, integrity checks pass, throughput within window) — and (B) the validation methods (automated count/checksum/field-level checks plus manual spot-checks) that will be used to test against those criteria. IP addresses (A) and API choices (D) are infrastructure/design, not test-plan content.

question: Universal Containers wants to introduce data volume testing to resolve ongoing performance defects earlier in the lifecycle. Regulations prohibit the use of production data in non-production environments. Which two options can the architect recommend? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Use Query Analyzer in production.
optionB: Request a partial sandbox copy after the next Salesforce release.
optionC: Generate mock data that mimics production data shape and volume.
optionD: Perform data masking on a Full sandbox after a refresh.
explanation: With production data prohibited downstream, two compliant approaches give realistic data volumes: (D) refresh a Full sandbox and then mask/anonymize the sensitive fields before any team uses it, and (C) generate synthetic data that mirrors production's row counts and distribution but contains no real PII. Both let performance testing run on production-like volumes without exposing regulated data.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is considering updating their Salesforce production deployment as a part of their release management process. Which three best practices should UC consider for production deployment? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Define a rollback strategy.
optionB: Announce the maintenance window ahead of time.
optionC: Alert all users on the day of deployment.
optionD: Temporarily suspend configuration changes in production.
explanation: Three production deployment best practices: (A) plan how to back out if something goes wrong; (B) communicate the maintenance window in advance so users can plan around it; (D) freeze production config during the deployment so admin/dev work doesn't conflict with the release. Same-day alerts (C) are too late, and aligning with Salesforce upgrades (E) is a separate concern (and usually undesirable).

question: Universal Containers (UC) is a high-tech company using SFDX tools and methodologies for its Salesforce development. UC has moved some of its code and configuration to unlocked packages. Which two best practices should an architect recommend to support UC's new package development strategy? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Test developed packages in test environments before installing to production.
optionB: Move everything in the existing codebase to a single monolithic package.
optionC: Consult the Metadata Coverage report to identify features supported by packages.
optionD: Version control does not need to be used, as packages manage all the code and configuration.
explanation: (A) Always validate packages by installing into staging/QA scratch orgs or sandboxes before production — packages can fail to install due to dependencies or environment differences. (C) The Metadata Coverage report tells you which metadata types are supported by unlocked packages (and which aren't), critical for splitting your codebase correctly. Monolithic packages (B) lose modular benefits; abandoning version control (D) is the opposite of best practice.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has been following the Waterfall methodology to deliver customer apps in Salesforce. As the business is growing at scale and with demand to incorporate features and functionality at faster pace, UC is finding the Waterfall approach is not optimal, and intends to transition towards an Agile development methodology. Which are two strengths of using an Agile development methodology? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Careful documentation is done at each step of the process so a target body of knowledge is available for inspection.
optionB: The project requirements in later phases are expected and accommodated by the process, by design.
optionC: All elements of the build are fully understood before work begins, reducing risk of unpleasant surprises.
optionD: There are many small releases of functional code, allowing stakeholders to see and touch the work in progress.
explanation: Agile's two signature strengths: (B) it embraces evolving requirements — change is expected and accommodated through reprioritization of the backlog; (D) it ships small increments frequently, giving stakeholders working software early and often. (A) heavy documentation and (C) full upfront understanding are Waterfall traits.

question: Ursa Major Solar (UMS) has used Aura components significantly in its Salesforce application development. UMS has established a robust test framework and the development team follows Salesforce-recommended testing practices. The UMS team uses Salesforce's test tool to check for common accessibility issues. In which two environments can the UMS team call Aura accessibility tests? Choose 2 answers
optionA: WebDriver Test
optionB: JS Test (Jasmine)
optionC: AuraDriver Test
optionD: ACCTEST
explanation: Salesforce provides $A.test.assertAccessible() / $A.test.getErrors() that work inside Aura JS tests (B), and the WebDriver-based UI testing layer (A) supports invoking the same accessibility checks during browser-driven tests. AuraDriver (C) and ACCTEST (D) are not actual Aura testing environments.

question: Cloud Kicks (CK) is launching a new sneaker line during the upcoming holiday season and needs to do thorough batch data testing before go-live. CK is using Salesforce Unlimited Edition. What two sandbox types should the architect recommend for batch data testing? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Developer Sandbox
optionB: Partial Copy Sandbox
optionC: Developer Pro Sandbox
optionD: Full Sandbox
explanation: Batch testing requires real data volumes. Only Partial Copy (B) and Full Copy (D) sandboxes carry production data — Partial via a sandbox template with up to 5 GB; Full as a complete replica. Developer (A, 200 MB) and Developer Pro (C, 1 GB) hold no production data and can't realistically test batch jobs.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has four different business units (BUs) with different processes that share global customers. They have implemented a multi-org strategy with one org consolidating customer 360-degree view, and four orgs for the different BUs. Each of the BU orgs reads and writes customer information from/to the customer 360-degree view org in real time. UC is now launching a new BU that will use Salesforce. It does not share customers with the other BUs and needs flexibility in their business processes. What should an architect recommend as org strategy for this new BU?
optionA: Use the same Salesforce org of another BU that shares geographical localization with the new BU.
optionB: Use a new Salesforce org for the new BU, and customize integration so that it reads and writes customer information from the customer data org.
optionC: Use a new stand-alone Salesforce org for the new BU, not integrated with the others.
optionD: Deploy the new BU in the customer 360-degree view org, and read/write customer information from it without need of custom integration.
explanation: The new BU has no shared customers with the other BUs and needs maximum flexibility — integrating it into the customer 360 hub would add complexity for zero benefit (no records to share). A new stand-alone org gives the BU full process autonomy. Reusing another BU's org (A) compromises both. Forcing integration (B, D) creates unnecessary coupling.

question: Universal Containers (UC) works with different partners and has few admin resources that take care of the day-to-day deployment tasks. As a result, UC would like to find a way to automate the deployments using Metadata API. Which two limitations of Metadata API should be considered when using Metadata API-based deployments? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Deploy and retrieve up to 10,000 files at once.
optionB: Maximum size of deployed .zip file is 400 MB.
optionC: Maximum size of deployed .zip file is 39 MB.
optionD: Deploy up to 10,000 files, but retrieve more than 10,000 files.
explanation: Per the Salesforce Metadata API limits: (A) up to 10,000 files per deploy/retrieve, and (B) a 400 MB compressed .zip size limit (post-decompression files have their own caps). The 39 MB figure (C) is the older base64-encoded inline payload limit for the legacy Metadata API SOAP envelope, not the file-based zip limit.

question: What are two limitations an architect should consider when designing a strategy for managing technical reference data with multiple related objects? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Apex CPU limits
optionB: Circular relationships
optionC: HTTP response size
optionD: Depth of nested relationships
explanation: (B) Salesforce does not allow circular reference chains in custom-metadata-style relationships, and any data design that produces them will fail to load or query cleanly. (D) SOQL limits the depth of relationship traversal — five levels of parent-to-child and five of child-to-parent — so deeply nested reference structures break querying. CPU (A) and HTTP response size (C) are runtime limits, not design-time data-model limits.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters (NTO) recently acquired Eastern Trail Outfitters (ETO). NTO's sales leadership team had hands-on experience with ETO's Sales Optimization app and have given the feedback that the app would benefit NTO's sales team. Which option should the architect recommend for having ETO's Sales Optimization app in NTO's Salesforce org in the shortest possible time?
optionA: Create an unmanaged package of the app and deploy in NTO's org.
optionB: Create users in ETO's org and provide access to NTO's sales team.
optionC: Create a core team and build the Sales Optimization app in NTO's org.
optionD: Create a managed package of the app and deploy in NTO's org.
explanation: A managed package built from the existing ETO app is the fastest way to ship the same code/config into NTO's org, with versioning and namespace isolation so it doesn't conflict with NTO's existing customizations. Creating users in ETO's org (B) puts NTO's reps on the wrong system. Rebuilding from scratch (C) is slowest. An unmanaged package (A) loses upgrade and namespace benefits but could also work — managed is the cleaner architect recommendation.

question: Universal Containers has just acquired Planetary Storage. Both companies use Salesforce to manage their sales activities. The two companies have many customers in common and the company plans to merge the two sales organizations, but the products and sales processes between the two original companies will remain different and distinct. What factor should the company consider in merging the two Salesforce orgs into a single org?
optionA: Transactional sales data could be combined without modification since standard objects are used.
optionB: Business processes on standard objects can be merged without modifications.
optionC: Customer data could be merged with modifications using standardization and de-duplication.
optionD: Salespersons selling both product lines would need two logins, one for each product line.
explanation: Many shared customers means shared customer records exist in both orgs with conflicting/duplicate data. The merge requires standardization (canonical formats for names, phones, addresses, IDs) and de-duplication (matching rules + duplicate rules + manual review). Sales data (A) and processes (B) cannot just be combined — they're different in each company. Two logins (D) defeats the purpose of merging.

question: Universal Containers has just initiated a project to implement a custom container tracking application with a large development team. The project manager is concerned that the large number of developers in a single Developer Pro sandbox could lead to challenges with code being overwritten. Which two methods should be used to mitigate this risk? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Replace the Developer Pro sandbox with a Partial Copy sandbox.
optionB: Provide each developer their own sandbox/developer org and implement a code repository and continuous integration to merge code into the Developer Pro sandbox.
optionC: Provide each developer their own sandbox/developer org and implement managed packages to deploy to the merge.
optionD: Use a single sandbox and strictly coordinate development across shared components, and implement a code repository to allow developers to merge code into a common repository.
explanation: (B) Per-developer environments + source control + CI is the standard isolated-development pattern: each developer works alone, commits to a branch, CI merges to a shared integration sandbox. (D) If single-environment work is unavoidable, strict coordination plus a shared code repository at least gives recoverability and merge resolution. (A) doesn't solve the conflict problem; (C) misuses managed packages.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a huge volume of metadata that cannot be deployed at once. What is the recommended strategy for UC to be successful with the deployment?
optionA: Use a continuous integration tool such as Jenkins to deploy in the right order.
optionB: Identify metadata dependencies, create logical groupings, and deploy in the right order.
optionC: Use a combination of the Ant Migration Tool and change sets for deployment.
optionD: Sort and group the metadata alphabetically and deploy them in the same order.
explanation: Large deployments fail because of dependency cycles and ordering — not because of the tool used. Map the dependency graph, partition metadata into deployable bundles (e.g., custom objects/fields → Apex → page layouts → reports), and deploy in dependency-respecting order. The choice of tool (A, C) is secondary; alphabetical ordering (D) ignores dependencies.

question: Universal Containers is about to begin the release of a major project. To facilitate this, they have several sandboxes to make their deployment train. These sandboxes are a mix of preview and non-preview instances. What should the architect recommend?
optionA: No advice needed, mixing instance types is important for regression testing.
optionB: Refresh all non-preview sandboxes during the release preview window.
optionC: Contact support to roll back the release when Salesforce upgrades the sandboxes.
optionD: Refresh all non-preview sandboxes when the release management team has time.
explanation: During the Salesforce release preview window, non-preview sandboxes still run the prior release while preview sandboxes are on the new release. Refreshing a non-preview sandbox during this window moves it onto a preview instance, putting all sandboxes on the new release together — preventing the pipeline from carrying components built against two different platform versions.

question: Universal Containers operates from North America and does business within North America. UC has just acquired a local company in Asia to start operating from Asia. Currently, these two business units operate in two different languages. Both units have different sales processes and have to comply strictly with local laws. During the expansion phase, UC would like to focus on innovation over standardization. What should an architect recommend given the scenario?
optionA: Single-org strategy, standardized sales process, common rules, and business-unit-specific locale.
optionB: Multi-org strategy, standardized sales process, common rules, and same locale across orgs.
optionC: Single-org strategy, standardized sales process, common rules, and same locale for all business units.
optionD: Multi-org strategy, each org has its own sales process, common rules, and operates in its own locale.
explanation: Different sales processes, different languages, strict differing legal compliance, and a stated preference for innovation over standardization all point to a multi-org strategy where each region runs its own processes and locale. Trying to standardize processes (A, B, C) contradicts the explicit business direction.

question: Universal Containers is validating an outbound change set from the Developer sandbox to the production org. Which two locking behaviors will occur during a deployment? Choose 2 answers
optionA: The sandbox org will be locked. Administrators cannot modify metadata.
optionB: The production org will be locked. Users will still be able to read/write data to the org.
optionC: The production org will be locked. Administrators cannot modify metadata during this time.
optionD: The production org will be locked. Users can only read data during this time.
explanation: During a deployment Salesforce locks metadata changes on the target org (C) — admins can't modify setup while the deployment is in flight — but users can continue to operate normally on data, both reading and writing (B). Data operations are not blocked, and the source sandbox is not locked (A).

question: Universal Containers (UC) is embarking on a large program of work, with different projects and different vendors. UC created a Center of Excellence (CoE) that is struggling with scope creep between the different projects. What role should the architect suggest be added to the CoE?
optionA: Change Manager
optionB: Release Manager
optionC: Product Owner
optionD: Scrum Master
explanation: Scope creep across projects is a backlog/priority/decision-rights problem. A Product Owner has formal authority to define, prioritize, and accept/reject items into a project's scope and to mediate competing requests across projects. Change Manager (A) handles user adoption; Release Manager (B) handles the deployment pipeline; Scrum Master (D) facilitates a single team and has no authority over scope.

question: Which two decisions should be made by an Architecture Review Board (ARB)? Choose 2 answers
optionA: What testing tools should be used to track integration testing requirements.
optionB: Whether to utilize the Waterfall or Agile methodology on the project.
optionC: Whether to implement Single Sign-On with SAML or delegated authentication.
optionD: Whether to create a new Salesforce object or override an existing object using a new record type.
explanation: An ARB makes architectural decisions: (C) authentication approach (SAML SSO vs. delegated authentication) is a security architecture choice; (D) data-model strategy (new object vs. record type on an existing object) is an architecture/data-model choice with long-term implications. Tool choice (A) is a tactical PMO call; methodology (B) belongs to the project sponsor / steering committee.

question: Universal Containers wants to implement a release strategy with major releases every four weeks and minor releases every week. Major releases follow Development, System Testing (SIT), User Acceptance Testing (UAT), and Training. Minor releases follow Development and User Acceptance Testing (UAT) stages. What represents a valid environment strategy consideration for UAT?
optionA: Minor and major releases use separate Developer Pro sandboxes.
optionB: Minor and major releases use the same Full Copy sandbox.
optionC: Minor releases use a Partial Copy sandbox and major releases use a Full Copy sandbox.
optionD: Minor releases use a Developer sandbox and major releases use a Full Copy sandbox.
explanation: Major releases need a production-like environment (Full Copy) for thorough UAT. Minor weekly releases are smaller in scope and can be UAT'd in a Partial Copy sandbox, which refreshes faster and is cheaper to maintain. Sharing a single Full Copy across both cadences (B) creates contention; Developer Pro (A) and Developer (D) lack the data needed for UAT.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a large backlog of work. They have noticed that despite their best efforts, valuable enhancements and updates are not being completed because they do not have the bandwidth to get them done. Many of these items are low effort and would not require experienced resources. What should the architect recommend that will minimize additional costs and allow UC to accomplish more?
optionA: UC should start a citizen development program.
optionB: UC should eliminate low-value items from the backlog.
optionC: UC should hire additional resources to reduce the backlog.
optionD: UC should hire a partner to complete the needed backlog items.
explanation: A citizen development program empowers business users (with light governance and guard-rails from IT) to build the low-effort, low-risk items themselves using declarative tools — clearing the backlog without growing the IT spend. Hiring (C) and partner engagement (D) increase costs; eliminating items (B) skips real value.

question: A technical lead is performing all code reviews for a team and is finding many errors and improvement points. This is delaying the team's deliveries. Which two actions can effectively contribute to the quality and agility of the team? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Create development standards and train teams in those standards.
optionB: Choose the most senior developer to help the technical lead in the code review.
optionC: Skip the code review and focus on functional tests and UAT.
optionD: Use a static code analysis tool in the pipeline before manual code review.
explanation: (A) Documented standards plus training shift many of those review findings 'left' — developers write better code the first time. (D) A static analyzer in the CI pipeline catches the mechanical issues automatically, so the human review can focus on logic and design. Skipping reviews (C) is an anti-pattern; loading another senior dev (B) doesn't address the root cause.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is looking for advice on how often it should refresh its sandboxes. UC currently uses a development lifecycle that starts with developer environments and moves to integration testing, QA testing, UAT, and then production. They have many scrum teams working concurrently and the teams do not agree on when refreshes should occur. What two recommendations should the architect suggest? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Integration sandboxes should be refreshed rarely because of the burden of maintaining the various API connections.
optionB: Sandboxes should be refreshed on the day when the refresh is allowed for that type of sandbox.
optionC: Development environments should generally be refreshed after each working feature has been successfully migrated.
optionD: Production is the only pristine environment.
explanation: (C) Refresh dev sandboxes after a feature ships so developers always start their next piece of work from the latest production-aligned state. (D) Treat production as the canonical source of truth — every other environment drifts and is rebuilt against production. Frequent integration refreshes break partner connections (A is sometimes argued, but C is more clearly a recommendation); calendar-driven refresh (B) ignores actual development flow.

question: Cloud Kicks is considering using an automated testing tool to help manage deployments between environments. When should the architect recommend the use of an automated testing tool?
optionA: Automated tests should be run daily in all developer orgs.
optionB: Automated tests should be run only when merging into Full or Partial Copy sandboxes.
optionC: Automated tests should be run daily in all developer orgs, and when branches are merged.
optionD: Automated tests should be run when branches are merged.
explanation: Best practice is to run automated tests both as a scheduled daily build (catches environment drift, scheduled-job interactions, regressions) AND on every merge to the integration branch (gates broken code from reaching shared environments). Restricting tests to merges into higher sandboxes only (B) lets bugs accumulate at lower tiers.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is developing a new Customer Community. Requirements for the Community are not fully defined. UC is planning on using an Agile methodology for this work and has promised delivery of the complete system in three months. What are two risks associated with this approach? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Agile does not allow for changes to requirements, so customers might not like the resulting solution.
optionB: Agile is not an appropriate development methodology for Customer Community implementations.
optionC: Given the lack of requirements and the three-month timeline commitment, the project may not be feasible.
optionD: The functionality that can be delivered in 3 months is unknown, and may not meet the needs of the business.
explanation: Agile fixes time/cost and flexes scope. With (C) requirements undefined and (D) a three-month commitment, what actually fits inside that window is unknowable today, so the team might deliver something that doesn't meet the business need. Agile absolutely supports requirement evolution (A is wrong) and works fine for Communities (B is wrong).

question: Universal Containers has decided on a single-org strategy, despite having to deal with the complexity of having multiple lines of business (LOBs) inside a single org. What are two common challenges in a single-org strategy for multiple LOBs? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Lack of declarative sharing and visibility capabilities to ensure correct visibility of objects and records.
optionB: Making Salesforce work with multiple currencies.
optionC: Apex design will need to be mature and adhere to strict guidelines to support a large enterprise model.
optionD: The data model becomes more complex as the scope in the org increases.
explanation: Two real single-org challenges: (D) the data model balloons as every LOB adds objects/fields, making the schema harder to understand and govern; (C) Apex must be tightly architected (trigger frameworks, separation of concerns, governor-aware patterns) to remain maintainable across LOBs sharing the same execution context. Sharing (A) and multi-currency (B) are well-supported declaratively.

question: Universal Containers (UC) operates globally from different geographical locations. UC is revisiting their current org strategy. Which three factors should an architect consider for a single-org strategy? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Consistent processes across the business.
optionB: Increased ability to collaborate.
optionC: Fewer interdependencies.
optionD: Centralized data location.
explanation: Single-org strategy is favored when: (A) the business runs consistent processes everywhere (one config fits all), (B) collaboration across regions is high (one org, one chatter, one search), and (D) centralized data is desired (a single 360 view of every customer). 'Fewer interdependencies' (C) and 'tailored implementation' (E) actually argue for multi-org.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a large user base (>300 users) and was originally implemented eight years ago by a Salesforce systems integration partner. Since then, UC has made a number of changes to their Visualforce pages and Apex classes in response to customer requirements, made by a variety of vendors and internal teams. Which three issues would a new technical architect expect to see when evaluating the code in the Salesforce org? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Custom-built JSON and string manipulation classes that are no longer required.
optionB: Multiple unit test failures would be encountered.
optionC: Broken functionality due to Salesforce upgrades.
optionD: Multiple triggers on the same object, making it hard to understand the order of operations.
optionE: Duplicated logic across Visualforce pages and Apex classes performing similar tasks.
explanation: Eight years of multi-vendor development typically produces (B) a brittle test suite with failing tests as the codebase has drifted, (D) the classic anti-pattern of multiple triggers per object with unclear ordering, and (E) duplicated logic between similar Visualforce/Apex artifacts because successive teams didn't refactor existing code. Salesforce-driven breakage (C) is rare due to backward compatibility; (A) is plausible but less universal than B/D/E.

question: Which are two characteristics of an effective communication plan? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Consistent communication to a pre-defined list of stakeholders.
optionB: Communication to stakeholders on a 'need-to-know' basis.
optionC: Requesting feedback for outstanding architectural questions.
optionD: Reporting project status, timelines, and impacts.
explanation: An effective communication plan is (A) regular and predictable — every named stakeholder knows when and what they'll receive — and (D) substantive — covering status, timelines, risks, and impacts. 'Need-to-know' (B) creates surprise and erodes trust. Architectural Q&A (C) is a working activity, not a hallmark of a good plan.

question: Universal Containers has just initiated a project involving a large distributed development and testing team. The development team members need access to a tool to manage requirements, and the testing team needs access to a tool to manage defects. Additionally, stakeholders are requesting ad-hoc status reports. What tool should an architect recommend to support the project?
optionA: Wave (Tableau CRM)
optionB: Code Repository
optionC: Spreadsheets
optionD: Project Management Tool
explanation: A modern Agile/project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps, etc.) handles all three needs in one place: requirements/user stories, defect tracking, and dashboards/reports for stakeholders. Code repos (B) hold code, not work items; analytics tools (A) and spreadsheets (C) don't manage work and lack workflow.

question: Universal Containers is in the process of testing their integration between Salesforce and their on-premise ERP systems. The testing team has requested a sandbox with up to 10,000 records in each object to benchmark the integration performance. What is the fastest approach an architect should recommend?
optionA: Spin off a Developer sandbox, migrate the metadata, and load the data using Data Loader.
optionB: Spin off a Full Copy sandbox with all the objects that are required for testing the integration.
optionC: Spin off a Developer Pro sandbox, migrate the metadata, and load the data using Data Loader.
optionD: Spin off a Partial Copy sandbox using a sandbox template with all the objects required for testing the integration.
explanation: A Partial Copy sandbox with a sandbox template is the fastest path: it provisions production metadata + the chosen objects' data automatically (no manual data loading) and only the objects defined by the template, capping at 5 GB. Full Copy (B) would work but is far slower to provision and refresh. Dev/Dev Pro (A, C) require manual data load.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is considering updating their Salesforce release management process. Which three best practices should UC consider for version control? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Maintain a single entry point for production from the master branch.
optionB: Maintain unrestricted access to the release sandboxes for all changes being deployed.
optionC: Maintain separate developer branches for minor and major releases.
optionD: Automation is a must with various application branches in the repository.
optionE: Maintain a single repository for the application with individual branches for projects.
explanation: Three version-control best practices: (A) one trunk/main branch is the single source of truth for production deploys; (C) separate branches isolate minor (fast-track) and major (longer-running) work so they don't entangle; (E) one repo per application with a branch per project keeps related artifacts together. Unrestricted sandbox access (B) is the opposite of governance, and 'automation is a must' (D) is true but not specifically a version-control practice.

question: Universal Containers (UC) uses a managed package to install an internal Sales app in five orgs. Within each org there are integrations and local processes with the objects of the managed package. UC wants to use unlocked packages for better integration with CI/CD processes. What would the Salesforce architect recommend for this migration?
optionA: Export all data from objects/fields of the managed package, uninstall it, install an unlocked package with the same namespace as the managed package, and restore all data.
optionB: Export all data from objects/fields of the managed package, uninstall it, install the unlocked package without a namespace, and restore all data.
optionC: Do not change to unlocked packages, as it is possible to perform all CI/CD processes with the managed package.
optionD: Migrate all classes, Visualforce, and components from the managed package to the unlocked package. Install the new version of the managed package (objects/fields only) and the unlocked package with the other components.
explanation: Reusing a managed namespace in an unlocked package (A) is not allowed. Uninstalling the managed package (A, B) destroys all dependent integrations and customizations across five orgs. The pragmatic path is (D): keep the managed package's data model (objects/fields) intact and migrate the code/UI components to an unlocked package, retaining all integrations and existing data while opening up CI/CD on the code layer.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters (NTO) has well-defined release management processes for both large and small projects. NTO's development team created a workflow and a trigger for changes in its opportunity renewal process. What should the architect recommend for release planning of these changes?
optionA: Plan this as an interim release after checking with Salesforce Support.
optionB: Plan this as a major release and align with a Salesforce major release.
optionC: Plan this as a minor release with training and change management.
optionD: Plan this as a patch release and align with a Salesforce patch release.
explanation: A workflow plus trigger affecting a sales process is small in scope but user-facing — sales reps will see the new behavior. A minor release with appropriate training and change management messaging is the right cadence. There is no need to align with Salesforce's release calendar (B, D); 'interim' releases (A) are not a defined NTO category.

question: What is the responsibility of the technical architect within a Change Control Board meeting?
optionA: Approve the upcoming release for deployment into production.
optionB: Prioritize the Salesforce product roadmap with business stakeholders.
optionC: Troubleshoot deployment errors that occurred during the last release.
optionD: Conduct code reviews for projects in the development phase.
explanation: In a CCB, the technical architect's seat exists to formally approve or reject the upcoming release on technical grounds — the architecture/quality gate for production deployment. Backlog prioritization (B), incident triage (C), and code reviews (D) all happen elsewhere.

question: Universal Containers wants to do weekly releases to production. What approach will mitigate the risk of bugs being inadvertently introduced to production?
optionA: Use of an Agile methodology.
optionB: Automated testing.
optionC: User acceptance testing.
optionD: Requirements traceability.
explanation: Weekly releases require a regression suite that runs in minutes, not days — manual UAT (C) and tracing (D) won't scale to that cadence. Automated testing (unit + integration + UI smoke) gates each release in a CI pipeline, blocking regressions before they reach production. Methodology (A) sets cadence but doesn't catch bugs.

question: Which two project situations favor a Waterfall methodology? Choose 2 answers
optionA: An application in post-production, with incremental changes made by a small team.
optionB: An application with many systems and inter-dependencies between components.
optionC: An application with regulatory compliance requirements to be validated by outside agencies.
optionD: An in-house application with a fixed team size, but an open timeline and flexible requirements.
explanation: (B) High inter-system dependencies need upfront design that maps interfaces, data flows, and sequencing — Waterfall's strength. (C) External regulatory validation needs the comprehensive documentation and traceable phase artifacts Waterfall produces. Post-production incremental work (A) and open-timeline/flexible-requirements work (D) are textbook Agile.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has received feedback from the field on several new feature requests aligned to business goals. UC is looking for a way to quickly get feedback and prioritize these requests. Which two options should an architect recommend? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Create the backlog or priority list in a project management tool.
optionB: Present the feature requests at a Center of Excellence meeting.
optionC: Send the requests to IT for a formal review at the end of the year.
optionD: Bring the feature request to the Test Manager to gain quality checks.
optionE: Create design standards around the new features being requested.
explanation: (A) A central backlog in a project management tool gives UC one place to capture, score, and rank requests against each other. (B) The Center of Excellence is the cross-functional governance body that reviews and prioritizes such requests against business goals — exactly the venue needed. Annual reviews (C) are too slow; QA (D) and design standards (E) aren't prioritization mechanisms.

question: Universal Containers has a highly integrated environment with significant process orchestration between systems. When refreshing UAT, objects that have External IDs from production no longer point to valid External IDs in the UAT environment. What should an architect do to resolve this?
optionA: Let UAT point to production integrations and roll back each transaction after it finishes.
optionB: Delete all the data and use an automated testing tool to create new data across all the systems in UAT.
optionC: Mask the External ID so nobody can see the production value.
optionD: In the post-refresh plan, modify External IDs to a known valid set of values for UAT.
explanation: Production External IDs reference real records in connected production systems — leaving them in UAT means UAT will accidentally reach into production-equivalent records on integrated systems. The standard post-refresh hygiene is to overwrite External IDs (and other system identifiers / endpoints / credentials) with values that map to UAT-equivalent records and endpoints.

question: What are two advantages of automated test data loads over manual data loads? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Automated loads can be done with no human oversight.
optionB: Automated loads are reliable in their results.
optionC: Automated loads will increase costs.
optionD: Automated loads cannot be scripted by CI/CD tools.
explanation: (A) Once scripted, automated loads run unattended (overnight, on schedule, in CI), no operator required. (B) They produce identical, repeatable results every run — a key property for testing. (C) is a disadvantage, not an advantage; (D) is false — CI/CD tools script Data Loader / Bulk API operations routinely.

question: The team at Universal Containers is building an application on Java that will interact with its Salesforce application. They want to use SOQL queries to retrieve and make changes to smaller pieces of Salesforce metadata through this application. Which API should the team leverage?
optionA: Tooling API
optionB: User Interface API
optionC: Metadata API
optionD: Any Salesforce API
explanation: Tooling API supports SOQL/SOSL against metadata objects (ApexClass, ApexTrigger, CustomField, etc.) for fine-grained, single-component operations — exactly what the question describes. Metadata API (C) is bulk, file-based, no SOQL. User Interface API (B) is for record-level UI building, not metadata.

question: What is the responsibility of an executive sponsor on a project?
optionA: Communicate project status.
optionB: Determine project methodology.
