optionC: Approve changes to project scope.
optionD: Design executive dashboards.
explanation: The executive sponsor owns the project's strategic alignment and budget, and therefore approves changes to scope (which affect both). Status communication (A) is the project manager's job; methodology (B) and dashboards (D) are operational decisions.

question: Universal Containers is about to begin development work on a new project in their Salesforce org that will take many months to complete. UC is concerned about how critical bugs will be addressed for existing live functionality. What is the recommended release management strategy to address this concern?
optionA: Include fixes for critical bugs in the ongoing development sandboxes so that they will be released with the other code.
optionB: Utilize a dedicated Developer Pro sandbox to address critical bugs and release to production.
optionC: Address critical bugs in the development sandboxes and push those changes to production separately.
optionD: Keep teams separate until the end of the project and create a Full Copy sandbox to merge their work then.
explanation: The standard pattern is a dedicated 'hotfix' branch and corresponding sandbox: hotfixes are isolated from in-flight feature work, are deployed straight to production on their own cadence, and are then merged back into the long-running feature branch so the new project picks up the same fix. This is exactly what option B describes.

question: Which statement is true for a Staging sandbox in a typical release pipeline?
optionA: Salesforce major releases (e.g., Winter to Spring) always occur in Staging and Production at the same time.
optionB: The Staging environment can only be updated once every two weeks.
optionC: The Staging sandbox is automatically refreshed on a schedule set by the administrator.
optionD: When created or refreshed, the Staging sandbox is a full replica of Production.
explanation: A Staging sandbox in a typical pipeline is a Full Copy sandbox specifically because it must mirror production data and configuration to provide a realistic final pre-prod environment. There is no auto-refresh on admin schedule (C) or 2-week update lock (B), and during preview windows Staging may be on a different platform release than Production (A).

question: Universal Containers (UC) is implementing a governance framework and has asked the architect to make recommendations regarding release planning. Which two decisions should the architect make when planning for releases? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Whether Salesforce will wait to upgrade the pod until after a UC release is complete.
optionB: When to test a new UC feature release if there are issues.
optionC: How to test existing functionality to ensure no regressions are introduced.
optionD: How to roll back to the previous Salesforce release if there are issues.
explanation: Two release-planning decisions belong to UC: (B) when to retest a feature release if defects are found (in-cycle re-test cadence), and (C) the regression strategy that proves nothing previously working has broken. UC cannot delay Salesforce's pod upgrades (A) or roll Salesforce back (D) — those aren't customer levers.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is working with Salesforce CPQ, which uses configuration sObjects to drive business logic. What are two best-practice recommendations an architect should propose to allow UC to deploy CPQ features as part of their CI/CD process? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Use Data Loader to deploy CSV files.
optionB: Build an Apex framework to deploy CPQ records.
optionC: Use a third-party product (e.g., Prodly, Copado).
optionD: Use an open-source SFDX plugin (e.g., sfdmu) and version control.
explanation: CPQ stores its configuration in records (Product, PriceBook, ProductRule, ProductOption, etc.), not in metadata, so it doesn't move via Metadata API. Best practice is to use purpose-built tooling: (C) third-party reference-data deployers (Prodly, Copado, Gearset) handle CPQ relationship-aware data deploys; (D) open-source SFDX plugins like sfdmu (SFDX Data Move Utility) script the same in CI under version control. Manual CSVs (A) and home-grown Apex (B) don't scale.

question: An architect is working on a Universal Containers project, and due to security concerns they cannot provide the architect with production access. Instead, a central release management team will be responsible for performing production deployments for all development teams. How should an architect leverage the Metadata API to ensure any metadata components necessary to deploy the project's functionality are properly communicated to the release management team?
optionA: Create a change set in each sandbox and download the package.xml file for the release management team.
optionB: Provide a spreadsheet of all components and utilize the Metadata API's readMetadata call.
optionC: Send a package.xml file with associated metadata in a .zip file to the release management team.
optionD: Provide the release management team a copy of the audit trail from the sandbox you wish to deploy from.
explanation: A package.xml manifest plus the corresponding metadata zip is the canonical Metadata API deployable. The release management team can drop the zip into their tooling (Workbench, Ant, SFDX) and deploy it directly into production. Change set XML (A) isn't portable; spreadsheets (B) and audit trails (D) aren't deployable artifacts.
